finding: A manipulation increasing sensory awareness of a specific domain (cleansing) caused shifts in moral judgment and stable social attitudes; the effect operated through heightened salience of that domain-specific value.
An action's attributed motivation reduced perceived morality of that action; perceived responsibility mediated the effect of motivation attribution on moral judgment.
Logically equivalent framings of the same comparative fact (a > b vs. b < a) produced asymmetric psychological effects; fluency explained the preference for one framing over the other.
Sensory fluency of a numerical attribute moderated the effect of decision context (feeling vs. cognition) on preferences; fluency-context match produced stronger evaluative effects.
An individual's stated philosophy about a fundamental aspect of experience correlated with well-being; more negative philosophies predicted lower well-being.
Gender differences in one psychological domain were partially replicated within a minority subpopulation; some domains showed effects of minority status.
A sensory stimulus increased attractiveness judgments when presented in a particular context; the effect was category-specific and unmediated by explicit awareness.
An environmental demographic cue activated risk-taking in a domain traditionally characterized by risk aversion; mating-relevant environmental stress reversed a typical choice pattern.
A Bayesian learning framework predicted generalization patterns from sparse examples; the model fit better than two competing account types.
Egocentrism in estimating others' knowledge was reduced when experience-based cues signaled that knowledge was unreliable; people shifted to more objective bases for prediction.
Desired outcomes altered perception of a basic physical attribute; the bias served a self-regulatory function.
Stereotypical associations with one demographic group generalized to younger members of that group in automatic categorization; explicit awareness was not required for the bias.
One clinical group showed elevated psychopathology across multiple dimensions compared to two control groups; group differences were substantial and consistent.

Psychiatric diagnosis alone did not distinguish individuals who attempted suicide from those who did not; subjective experience and behavioral tendencies, combined with developmental history, predicted suicide attempts.
The effects of individual differences on adjustment were mediated by coping strategies; coping methods explained the mechanism linking traits to outcomes.
Positive affect and broad-minded coping reciprocally reinforced one another over time; positive affect initiated a self-sustaining spiral.
Spirituality comprises multiple dimensions; these dimensions relate to but are distinct from a standard personality model.
Multiple distinct patterns of positive outcomes were identifiable; each pattern was predicted by different configurations of emotional and identity processes.
Trait measures predicted behavioral criteria consistently across different assessment instruments; predictions replicated across independent samples.
Individual traits predicted satisfaction in intimate relationships; different traits were relevant in different relationship types.
